items.foco: Triad of symptoms: diarrhea, rectal bleeding, straining and tenesmus
items.title: Classic symptomatic triad
items.contentHtml:
Triad of symptoms: diarrhea, rectal bleeding, straining and tenesmus

  • The triad of symptoms includes diarrhea (with mucus and blood), rectal bleeding and straining and tenesmus (feeling of incomplete evacuation and painful effort).
  • Pushing is the urgent desire to defecate; Tenesmus is rectal pain associated with muscle contraction. In the differential diagnosis with Crohn's disease, the absence of severe abdominal pain and fever suggests ulcerative colitis. Rectal bleeding is the most specific symptom and is usually the reason for consultation.

items.foco: Continuous mucosal lesion from proximal rectum
items.title: Extension and continuity of the injury
items.contentHtml:
Continuous mucosal lesion from the proximal rectum

  • The involvement is continuous from the proximal rectum, without areas of intervening healthy mucosa (unlike Crohn's).
  • The extension is classified as: proctitis (rectum only), left colitis (up to the splenic flexure) and extensive colitis/pancolitis (beyond the angle splenic).
  • proctitis represents ~30% of cases at diagnosis; pancolitis ~20%.
  • The inflammation is limited to the mucosa and submucosa, rarely involving deep layers.

items.foco: Inflammatory pseudopolyps as an endoscopic finding
items.title: Inflammatory pseudopolyps
items.contentHtml:
Inflammatory pseudopolyps as an endoscopic finding

  • The inflammatory pseudopolyps are projections of regenerative mucosa surrounded by ulcers, typical of chronic ulcerative colitis.
  • Endoscopically they appear as sessile polyps or reddish pedunculated polyps, not neoplastic.
  • Their presence indicates disease. chronicand irregular healing; they do not require resection.
  • Differential diagnosis: adenomatous polyps; the biopsy confirms inflammation without dysplasia.

items.title: Key histological findings
items.contentHtml:
Histology: cryptitis and cryptic abscesses

  • Cryptitis is the inflammation of the crypts of Lieberkühn, with infiltration of neutrophils.
  • Cryptic abscesses are accumulations of neutrophils within the lumen of the crypts, a characteristic finding.
  • Other findings: distortion of cryptic architecture (bifurcated, shortened crypts) and Paneth cell metaplasia in the left colon.
  • dysplasia is associated with chronicity >8-10 years.

items.title: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC)
items.contentHtml:
Association with primary sclerosing cholangitis

  • Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is a hepatobiliary complication that affects up to 5% of patients with ulcerative colitis. ERCP.
  • The risk of cholangiocarcinoma is increased; Annual cholangio-MRI is recommended in patients with PSC.
  • Ulcerative colitis associated with PSC is usually pancolitis and milder, but with a higher risk of colorectal cancer.

items.foco: Increased risk of colorectal cancer after 8-10 years
items.title: Colorectal cancer (CRC) risk
items.contentHtml:
Increased risk of colorectal cancer after 8-10 years

  • The risk of colorectal cancer increases after 8-10 years of disease, especially in pancolitis.
  • Surveillance colonoscopy begins 8 years after diagnosis, with serial biopsies every 1-2 cm (colonoscopy protocol). Seattle).
  • High-grade dysplasia is an indication for proctocolectomy.
  • Additional risk factors: primary sclerosing cholangitis, family history of CRC, and persistent active inflammation.

items.foco: First line in mild-moderate: oral and topical mesalazine
items.title: First line treatment
items.contentHtml:
First-line in mild-moderate: oral and topical mesalazine

  • Mesalazine (5-ASA) is the first-line drug for mild to moderate disease.
  • Oral route: dose of 2-4 g/day (e.g., mesalazine 2.4 g/day in release prolonged).
  • Topical route: enemas or suppositories of mesalazine 1 g/day for proctitis or left colitis.
  • The oral + topical combination is superior to monotherapy for induction of remission.

items.foco: Corticosteroids in moderate to severe outbreak
items.title: Corticosteroids in acute outbreak
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids in moderate to severe outbreak

  • Corticoids are indicated in moderate to severe outbreaks that do not respond to 5-ASA.
  • Oral route: prednisone 40-60 mg/day with gradual reduction (no more than 8-12 weeks).
  • Via intravenous: hydrocortisone 300-400 mg/day or methylprednisolone 40-60 mg/day for severe flares.
  • Do not use corticosteroids as maintenance; seek to save steroids with immunomodulators or biologicals.

items.foco: Infliximab or adalimumab in refractory disease
items.title: Anti-TNF biological therapy
items.contentHtml:
Infliximab or adalimumab in refractory disease

  • Infliximab (anti-TNF monoclonal antibody) is administered IV: 5 mg/kg in weeks 0, 2, 6 and then every 8 weeks.
  • Adalimumab (subcutaneous anti-TNF): loading dose 160 mg, then 80 mg in week 2, and 40 mg every 2 weeks.
  • Indicated in refractory disease to corticosteroids or immunomodulators, or in moderate to severe disease.
  • Contraindications: undrained abscess, active tuberculosis or heart failure NYHA III-IV.

items.foco: Surgery: restorative proctocolectomy with ileal pouch
items.title: Curative surgery
items.contentHtml:
Surgery: restorative proctocolectomy with ileal pouch

  • Restorative proctocolectomy with ileal pouch (ileal J-pouch) is the surgery of choice for ulcerative colitis.
  • Indications: disease refractory to medical treatment, dysplasia or cancer, Toxic megacolon and massive hemorrhage.

items.title: Toxic megacolon
items.contentHtml:
Toxic megacolon: urgent surgical complication

  • Toxic megacolon is a serious complication defined by dilatation of the colon >6 cm (or >5.5 cm in the cecum) with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, dehydration, altered sensorium).
  • Criteria for Jalan include: fever >38.5°C, HR >120 bpm, leukocytosis >10,500/mm³, anemia and dehydration.
  • Initial management: decompression with nasogastric tube, IV corticosteroids and antibiotherapy (metronidazole + ciprofloxacin).
  • Lack of response in 24-48 hours or perforation indicate urgent subtotal colectomy.

items.foco: Iron deficiency anemia due to chronic blood loss
items.title: Iron deficiency anemia
items.contentHtml:
Iron deficiency anemia due to chronic blood loss

  • Iron deficiency anemia is the most common hematological manifestation, secondary to chronic blood loss due to the inflamed mucosa.
  • Diagnosis: serum ferritin <30 ng/mL (or <100 ng/mL if active inflammation) with ferritin saturation transferrin <20%.
  • Treatment: oral iron (ferrous sulfate 100-200 mg/day) or IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if oral intolerance or severe anemia (Hb <10 g/dL).
  • Correction of anemia improves quality of life and reduces the need for transfusions.
